lesson.name: Marx: Communism & Political Economy - Lesson 1 — Manifesto of the Communist Party

meta.lessonObjective: Class struggle and the materialist conception of history.

messages.content:
Lesson 1 — Manifesto of the Communist Party - Presentation26 Slides
The History of All Societies
  • The history of all hitherto existing societies is the history of class struggles.
  • Freeman and slave, patrician and plebeian, lord and serf, guild-master and journeyman stood in constant opposition to one another and carried on an uninterrupted fight.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the Manifesto, what is the history of all hitherto existing societies?
questions.explanation: Page 1 states directly: 'The history of all hitherto existing societies is the history of class struggles.'

questions.question: What distinctive feature does the Manifesto attribute to the bourgeois epoch?
options: It has simplified class antagonisms into two great classes
It has restored medieval gradations of rank
It has eliminated all forms of oppression
It has returned society to feudal relations
questions.explanation: Page 1 notes that bourgeois society 'has simplified the class antagonisms' into bourgeoisie and proletariat.

questions.question: Why did feudal relations of property become fetters, according to the text?
options: They became incompatible with the developed productive forces
They were destroyed by foreign invasion
They were abolished by royal decree
They prevented the rise of religion
questions.explanation: Page 3 explains that feudal relations 'became no longer compatible with the already developed productive forces' and had to be burst asunder.

questions.question: Which class alone does the Manifesto call a really revolutionary class today?
questions.explanation: Page 6 states: 'Of all the classes that stand face to face with the bourgeoisie today, the proletariat alone is a really revolutionary class.'

questions.question: What does the Manifesto say the bourgeoisie produces above all?
questions.explanation: Page 6 concludes: 'What the bourgeoisie therefore produces, above all, is its own grave-diggers.'

cards.front: What does the Manifesto identify as the history of all societies?
cards.back: The history of class struggles between oppressor and oppressed.

cards.front: How does bourgeois society differ from earlier epochs in its class structure?
cards.back: It simplifies antagonisms into two great classes: bourgeoisie and proletariat.

cards.front: What must the bourgeoisie constantly do to exist?
cards.back: Constantly revolutionise the instruments and relations of production.

cards.front: What phrase describes the effect of bourgeois revolutionising on fixed relations?
cards.back: All that is solid melts into air.

cards.front: Why did feudal property relations become fetters?
cards.back: They became incompatible with the already developed productive forces.

cards.front: Which class alone is described as truly revolutionary under modern industry?
cards.back: The proletariat, as the essential product of modern industry.

cards.front: What replaces the isolation of labourers caused by competition?
cards.back: Their revolutionary combination through association.

cards.front: Why is the bourgeoisie unfit to remain the ruling class?
cards.back: It cannot assure an existence to its slave within his slavery.

cards.front: What does the Manifesto say all previous historical movements were?
cards.back: Movements of minorities or in the interests of minorities.

cards.front: What outcome does the Manifesto declare inevitable?
cards.back: The fall of the bourgeoisie and the victory of the proletariat.

slides.title: The History of All Societies
bullets: The history of all hitherto existing societies is the history of class struggles.
Freeman and slave, patrician and plebeian, lord and serf, guild-master and journeyman stood in constant opposition to one another and carried on an uninterrupted fight.
This ceaseless opposition has always propelled entire epochs forward by overturning old ways of producing and owning, until the ruling group can no longer contain the forces it itself has unleashed.

Each victory of one side therefore plants the contradictions that will ignite the next round of struggle, showing that every social order carries within it the conditions of its own eventual overthrow.
slides.notes: Opens the Manifesto with the core thesis on page 1.

slides.title: Outcomes of Every Class Struggle
bullets: Each time the fight ended either in a revolutionary re-constitution of society at large or in the common ruin of the contending classes.
This pattern holds across all earlier epochs of history.
When one class prevails it does not merely seize power but installs a new mode of production that rearranges property, labor, and all social relations for centuries to come.
When both classes destroy each other the productive powers they once commanded lie in ruins, and the survivors inherit only stagnation or outright regression.
slides.notes: States the two possible results of class conflict from page 1.

slides.title: Ancient and Medieval Class Orders
bullets: In ancient Rome we find patricians, knights, plebeians and slaves; in the Middle Ages, feudal lords, vassals, guild-masters, journeymen, apprentices and serfs.
These societies displayed a complicated arrangement of various orders and manifold gradations of social rank.
These ranks rested on concrete control of land, tools and labor, so that each order extracted wealth from the one below and defended its privileges against those above.

The resulting frictions, whether plebeians pressing for land against patrician monopoly or journeymen chafing under guild restrictions, already contained the seeds of open collision that would later reshape entire epochs.
slides.notes: Describes pre-modern complexity on page 1.

slides.title: Bourgeois Society Replaces Feudal Orders
bullets: Modern bourgeois society that sprouted from the ruins of feudal society has not done away with class antagonisms but established new classes, new conditions of oppression and new forms of struggle.
Our epoch possesses the distinctive feature that it has simplified the class antagonisms.

Where feudal ranks once layered society with countless intermediate stations and local loyalties, the new order reduces every relation to the stark encounter between those who command the factories and those who must sell their working hours to survive.

This narrowing of the field of battle concentrates all social tension at a single point, so that the daily clash over wages and working time becomes the decisive lever that can overturn the entire system of ownership.
slides.notes: Shows the transition from page 1.

slides.title: Society Splits into Two Hostile Camps
bullets: Society as a whole is more and more splitting up into two great hostile camps, into two great classes directly facing each other: Bourgeoisie and Proletariat.
This simplification replaces the old gradations with a single, fundamental opposition.
Every intermediate rank from artisan to peasant is crushed or absorbed, so the battlefield narrows until capital and wage labor stand alone with nothing between them.
The resulting naked opposition turns every economic crisis into a political confrontation in which the proletariat discovers both its misery and its power to abolish the whole system of classes.
slides.notes: Names the modern binary on page 1.

slides.title: Bourgeoisie Emerges from Medieval Towns
bullets: From the serfs of the Middle Ages sprang the chartered burghers of the earliest towns, and from these burgesses the first elements of the bourgeoisie were developed.
The discovery of America and the rounding of the Cape opened up fresh ground for this rising class.
These towns became islands of money-based exchange inside a world still ruled by land and personal bondage, allowing merchants to accumulate wealth that no feudal lord could fully seize.
The new oceanic routes multiplied markets and profits so rapidly that the bourgeoisie gained the economic power to reshape laws, governments, and social relations in its own interest.
slides.notes: Traces bourgeois origins on page 1.

slides.title: Markets and Manufacture Transform Production
bullets: The discovery of America, the East-Indian and Chinese markets, colonisation and the increase in the means of exchange gave commerce, navigation and industry an impulse never before known.
The feudal system of industry monopolised by closed guilds no longer sufficed for the growing wants of the new markets.
These new markets therefore shattered the old guild limits on output and quality, forcing production into larger workshops where tasks were split among many hands to meet the rising flood of demand.

The result was a growing body of propertyless workers whose daily labor now depended on distant buyers rather than local custom, sharpening the divide between those who controlled capital and those who sold only their capacity to work.
slides.notes: Explains early capitalist expansion on page 1.

slides.title: Steam and Machinery Create Modern Industry
bullets: Steam and machinery revolutionised industrial production and the place of manufacture was taken by giant Modern Industry.
The place of the industrial middle class was taken by industrial millionaires, the leaders of whole industrial armies, the modern bourgeois.
Factories driven by steam gathered thousands of workers under one roof, stripping them of independent craft skills and binding their daily existence to the relentless pace of the machine.

This centralization of production handed decisive economic power to a handful of industrial millionaires, sharpening their rivalry with one another while placing the mass of propertyless laborers in direct opposition to their rule.
slides.notes: Marks the industrial revolution on page 2.

slides.title: Bourgeoisie Develops Through Revolutions in Production
bullets: We see how the modern bourgeoisie is itself the product of a long course of development, of a series of revolutions in the modes of production and of exchange.
Each step in the development of the bourgeoisie was accompanied by a corresponding political advance of that class.

These upheavals in production continually forced merchants and manufacturers to shatter older guild restrictions and feudal land ties, concentrating capital in their hands while pulling distant regions into worldwide markets.

Each political gain, whether parliamentary seats or laws safeguarding property, therefore sharpened a fresh antagonism because the same revolutions that elevated the bourgeoisie also multiplied the numbers of wage laborers whose collective power would one day confront them.
slides.notes: Links economic change to political power on page 2.

slides.title: Bourgeoisie Constantly Revolutionises Production
bullets: The bourgeoisie cannot exist without constantly revolutionising the instruments of production, and thereby the relations of production, and with them the whole relations of society.
Conservation of the old modes of production in unaltered form was the first condition of existence for all earlier industrial classes.

This compulsion to innovate means that entire branches of industry are periodically destroyed and replaced, throwing masses of workers into new forms of dependence while old skills and communities are swept aside.

Unlike earlier classes that could preserve their position through rigid tradition, the bourgeoisie must therefore generate endless social upheaval simply to survive, turning every fixed relation into a fleeting transaction.
slides.notes: States the dynamic law of bourgeois existence on page 3.

slides.title: All Fixed Relations Melt into Air
bullets: Constant revolutionising of production, uninterrupted disturbance of all social conditions and everlasting uncertainty distinguish the bourgeois epoch from all earlier ones.
All fixed, fast-frozen relations with their train of ancient and venerable prejudices and opinions are swept away.

This ceaseless churning of production drags every social tie into the marketplace, leaving workers and capitalists alike stripped of inherited obligations and forced to renegotiate their existence daily through wages and contracts.

The result is that class antagonism stands naked, no longer softened by ancient loyalties, so that the struggle between those who own the new productive forces and those who operate them becomes the driving motor of historical change itself.
slides.notes: Quotes the famous dissolution on page 3.

slides.title: All That Is Solid Melts into Air
bullets: All that is solid melts into air, all that is holy is profaned, and man is at last compelled to face with sober senses his real conditions of life and his relations with his kind.
This sentence describes how the bourgeoisie strips away every traditional veil from social relations.
Once traditions lose their power to mask daily life, workers must reckon directly with the fact that their survival depends on selling labor to those who control the factories and the land.

This demystification turns the hidden engine of history, class antagonism rooted in production, into an open battlefield where the exploited can at last organize to abolish the very system that stripped their illusions away.
slides.notes: Explains the materialist unveiling on page 3.

slides.title: World Market and Cosmopolitan Production
bullets: The need of a constantly expanding market chases the bourgeoisie over the whole surface of the globe and gives a cosmopolitan character to production and consumption in every country.
National industries are dislodged by new industries that draw raw material from the remotest zones and sell products in every quarter of the globe.

This global chase for markets stitches together far-flung societies so that a factory closure in one region immediately throws workers elsewhere into unemployment, exposing how capital treats every population as interchangeable fuel for its expansion.

The resulting interdependence dissolves the protective barriers once maintained by local guilds and tariffs, leaving communities everywhere exposed to the same price fluctuations and compelling them to recognize their shared position beneath a single, borderless system of exploitation.
slides.notes: Shows global expansion on page 3.

slides.title: Bourgeoisie Creates a World After Its Own Image
bullets: The bourgeoisie compels all nations, on pain of extinction, to adopt the bourgeois mode of production and to become bourgeois themselves.
In one word, it creates a world after its own image.
This global compulsion replaces ancient customs and local crafts with factories, railroads, and wage contracts, binding every region into one vast circuit of buying and selling.
The result is a single world market in which national boundaries survive only as lines on a map while real power concentrates in the hands of those who own the machines and the credit.
slides.notes: States the universalising drive on page 3.

slides.title: Bourgeoisie Concentrates Property and Centralises the State
bullets: The bourgeoisie keeps doing away with the scattered state of the population, of the means of production and of property, agglomerating production and concentrating property in a few hands.
The necessary consequence was political centralisation into one nation with one government and one national class-interest.

This process forges a single national market in which every local barrier falls before the drive for profit, binding distant regions into one circuit of production and exchange that no feudal patchwork could sustain.

The resulting state machine therefore stands openly as the executive committee of the possessing class, replacing the old tangle of privileges with uniform laws that clear the path for capital while sharpening the line between those who own and those who must sell their labour.
slides.notes: Links concentration to the nation-state on page 3.

slides.title: Productive Forces Surpass All Previous History
bullets: During its rule of scarce one hundred years the bourgeoisie has created more massive and more colossal productive forces than have all preceding generations together.
Subjection of Nature’s forces, machinery, steam-navigation, railways and electric telegraphs are products of social labour never before imagined.

These colossal instruments now stand ready to abolish material scarcity for the whole of humanity yet remain chained to the pursuit of private gain so that abundance itself becomes a source of crisis and waste.

Because the new productive powers concentrate social labor on a planetary scale they also gather the working class into vast disciplined formations capable of grasping and redirecting those same powers once the existing property relations are swept away.
slides.notes: Quantifies the productive leap on page 3.

slides.title: Feudal Relations Become Fetters on Production
bullets: At a certain stage the feudal relations of property became no longer compatible with the already developed productive forces and they became so many fetters.
They had to be burst asunder and into their place stepped free competition and the economical and political sway of the bourgeois class.

The rising merchants and manufacturers therefore rallied peasants and artisans behind them, converting blocked workshops and toll-ridden roads into battle lines that toppled noble privilege and absolute monarchy alike.

With the old restraints shattered, production surged under the banner of free competition, yet the same victory immediately installed a new master: the wage relation that now pits the propertyless multitude against the owners of capital.
slides.notes: Describes the contradiction that ended feudalism on page 3.

slides.title: The Same Movement Now Unfolds Before Our Eyes
bullets: A similar movement is going on before our own eyes in modern bourgeois society.
The gigantic means of production and exchange the bourgeoisie has conjured up now turn against the very relations that generated them.

These forces now generate recurrent crises of overproduction in which goods pile up unsold while workers stand idle, exposing how private appropriation clashes with socialized production. The resulting instability drives the bourgeoisie to intensify exploitation and centralize capital, sharpening the very class antagonisms that threaten its rule.
Out of this conflict emerges a proletariat disciplined by the factory system itself, whose collective interest lies in abolishing the property relations that now fetter further advance.
slides.notes: Applies the same logic to the present on page 4.

slides.title: Proletariat Is the Only Truly Revolutionary Class
bullets: Of all the classes that stand face to face with the bourgeoisie today, the proletariat alone is a really revolutionary class.
The other classes decay and finally disappear in the face of Modern Industry; the proletariat is its special and essential product.

Because wage laborers possess nothing but their capacity to work, their struggle cannot stop at partial reforms or guild privileges and must instead sweep away the whole machinery of bourgeois ownership.

Every other social layer ultimately seeks to rescue or adapt its own fragment of property, yet the proletariat alone finds its daily existence turned into an unrelenting pressure to abolish property itself and thereby end class rule.
slides.notes: Identifies the revolutionary subject on page 6.

slides.title: Proletarians Have No Property to Defend
bullets: All previous classes that got the upper hand sought to fortify their already acquired status by subjecting society to their conditions of appropriation.
The proletarians cannot become masters of the productive forces except by abolishing their own previous mode of appropriation and thereby every other previous mode.

Because workers own no instruments or land of their own, their seizure of power cannot defend an existing property form and must instead dismantle every title by which anyone has previously commanded the labor of others.

This necessity turns the proletarian revolution into the first movement that extinguishes class rule altogether, freeing the productive forces from the narrow limits of private appropriation so they may serve the full development of every human being.
slides.notes: Explains why proletarian revolution abolishes property on page 6.

slides.title: The Proletarian Movement Is the Movement of the Immense Majority
bullets: All previous historical movements were movements of minorities or in the interests of minorities.
The proletarian movement is the self-conscious, independent movement of the immense majority, in the interests of the immense majority.

Because the proletariat forms the overwhelming bulk of society its aims cannot be smuggled in by a handful of conspirators but must be fought out in broad daylight where every factory gate and every wage packet becomes a site of open reckoning.

This majority character turns the struggle into a decisive break with all earlier epochs since no minority can any longer pose as the representative of the whole and the old state machine therefore stands exposed as an instrument that must be destroyed rather than merely seized.
slides.notes: Contrasts minority and majority movements on page 6.

slides.title: Bourgeoisie Is Unfit to Rule Because It Cannot Feed Its Slaves
bullets: It becomes evident that the bourgeoisie is unfit any longer to be the ruling class because it is incompetent to assure an existence to its slave within his slavery.
Society can no longer live under this bourgeoisie; its existence is no longer compatible with society.

The worker therefore confronts daily hunger and insecurity not as accidents of fortune but as the direct result of a system whose drive for profit leaves no room for steady subsistence, sharpening the antagonism until open revolt becomes the only remaining outlet.

This contradiction between expanding productive powers and the narrowing social relations that contain them marks the point at which one epoch of history yields to the next, as the class whose labor sustains everything finally asserts its power to reorganize society on a new foundation.
slides.notes: Shows the ruling class’s incapacity on page 6.

slides.title: Wage-Labour Competition Is Replaced by Revolutionary Combination
bullets: The advance of industry replaces the isolation of the labourers, due to competition, by their revolutionary combination, due to association.
The development of Modern Industry therefore cuts from under its feet the very foundation on which the bourgeoisie produces and appropriates products.
Factories therefore become the very places where scattered hands discover they share one enemy and one destiny, turning daily cooperation at the machine into the discipline of collective revolt.
The bourgeoisie thus finds its own relentless drive for profit assembling the only force capable of ending wage labour altogether and seizing the productive apparatus it once commanded.
slides.notes: Names the mechanism that produces the grave-diggers on page 6.

slides.title: What the Bourgeoisie Produces Above All Is Its Own Grave-Diggers
bullets: What the bourgeoisie therefore produces, above all, is its own grave-diggers.
Its fall and the victory of the proletariat are equally inevitable.
Every advance in machinery and global trade herds workers into vast factories and cities where they discover their common position and begin to act as a single class.
The same competitive pressures that enrich the owners therefore sharpen the very antagonisms that will bring the entire order down.
slides.notes: Concludes the materialist prediction on page 6.

questions.question: According to the Manifesto, what is the history of all hitherto existing societies?
questions.explanation: Page 1 states directly: 'The history of all hitherto existing societies is the history of class struggles.'

options: It has simplified class antagonisms into two great classes
It has restored medieval gradations of rank
It has eliminated all forms of oppression
It has returned society to feudal relations

options: They became incompatible with the developed productive forces

They prevented the rise of religion

slides.title: Flashcards

cards.front: What does the Manifesto identify as the history of all societies?

cards.back: It simplifies antagonisms into two great classes: bourgeoisie and proletariat.

cards.back: All that is solid melts into air.
